In this secti on, you will learn to:  
 Identify danger signs.  
 Identify signs of illness (that are not danger signs).  
 Decide if the child must be referred to the health facility or whether you can treat the 
child in the community.  
Any DANGER SIGN: Refer the child  
On the r ecording form, the middle column —Any DANGER SIGN? —lists the danger signs. 
[Find the column that lists the danger signs.]  
Any one of these signs is a reason to refer the child URGENTLY to the health facility. Using 
the information you have about the child,  tick [] any danger sign or signs you find.  
The first seven danger signs are found by asking the caregiver about the child’s problems.  
 Cough for 14  days or more  
A child who has had cough for 14 days or more has a danger sign. The child may have 
tubercu losis (TB), asthma, whooping cough, or another problem. The child needs more 
assessment and treatment at the health facility. Refer a child with cough for 14 days or 
more.

diagnosis.diagnosis: Based on the medical guide information provided, the patient's symptoms of cough for 2 days do not meet the criteria for the conditions explicitly outlined for urgent referral, such as cough for 14 days or more, which may suggest tuberculosis (TB), asthma, whooping cough, or another problem requiring further assessment and treatment at a health facility. The guide does not provide specific conditions for a cough lasting less than 14 days without other danger signs or symptoms. Therefore, without additional symptoms or danger signs listed in the guide, it's not possible to determine the likely conditions explaining the patient's symptoms of a cough for 2 days based solely on the medical guide information provided.
diagnosis.treatment:
Immediate Care Steps:
The medical guide does not provide specific immediate care steps for a patient with a cough for 2 days.

Home Care:
For a patient with a cough for 2 days, the medical guide advises the following home care instructions:

- Do not give cough medicine to the child, as cough medicines can be expensive and often contain ingredients that are harmful for children.
- Warm water with honey will be comforting for a child with a cough and may be all that the child needs.
- Advise the caregiver to go to the nearest health facility if the child becomes sicker, which means that the medicine is not working or the child has another problem.
- It is urgent to seek care immediately if the child cannot drink or feed, becomes sicker, or has blood in the stool.
- The caregiver should be aware of signs that the child is not getting better, such as becoming weaker and very sleepy, or if a child with a cough has difficulty breathing.

These instructions should be followed as outlined to ensure the well-being of the child with a cough.

Referral Guidance:
The medical guide does not provide specific referral criteria for a patient with a cough for 2 days. It specifies that a child with a cough for 14 days or more has a danger sign and needs to be referred urgently to a health facility for further assessment and treatment. Therefore, based on the information provided in the guide, a cough for 2 days does not meet the criteria for urgent referral.

nd medicines recommended by your doctor/ health care provider.

to keep  up your milk supply and prevent breast difficulties.  
Increase the frequency of breastfeeding and breastfeed for longer at each feed , to help your 
baby regain health and weight.

breast when offered.  
When you are sick, you can continue to breastfeed your baby. You may need extra fluids and  
support during this time yourself.

rice water, and yoghurt drinks will help to replace the lost fluid during illness. The child with

 Becomes sicker  
 Has blood in the stool  
Usually a caregiver will know when a child is improving or becoming sicker. Ask the caregiver 
what she will look for. A child may become weaker and very sleepy. A child with a cough may 
have  difficulty  breathing.  Make sure that the caregiver recognizes when the child is not 
get

nd medicines recommended by your doctor/ health care provider.  
If the baby is too weak to suckle, express breast milk to give the baby. This will also help you 
to keep  up your milk supply and prevent breast difficulties.  
Increase the frequency of breastfeeding and breastfeed for longer at each feed , to help your 
baby regain health and weight.  
Do not give any throat or cough remedy. A child, even with a sore throat, will usually take the 
breast when offered.  
When you are sick, you can continue to breastfeed your baby. You may need extra fluids and  
support during this time yourself.  
For children who are not exclusively breastfed, give clean water and more fluid foods. Soup, 
rice water, and yoghurt drinks will help to replace the lost fluid during illness. The child with 
diarrhoea should also take ORS solution.

On the r ecording form, the middle column —Any DANGER SIGN? —lists the danger signs. 
[Find the column that lists the danger signs.]  
Any one of these signs is a reason to refer the child URGENTLY to the health facility. Using 
the information you have about the child,  tick [] any danger sign or signs you find.  
The first seven danger signs are found by asking the caregiver about the child’s problems.  
 Cough for 14  days or more  
A child who has had cough for 14 days or more has a danger sign. The child may have 
tubercu losis (TB), asthma, whooping cough, or another problem. The child needs more 
assessment and treatment at the health facility. Refer a child with cough for 14 days or 
more.

  Diarrhoea for 14 days or more  
Diarrhoea often stops on its own in 3 or 4 days. Diarrhoea for 14 days or more, however, is a 
danger sign. It may be a sign of a severe disease. The diarrhoea will contribute to malnutrition. 
Diarrhoea also can cause dehydration, when the body loses more f luids than are being 
replaced. If not treated, dehydration results in death. Refer a child with diarrhoea for 14 
days or more.  
 
 Blood in stool  
Diarrhoea with blood in the stool, with or without mucus, is dysentery. If there is blood in the 
stool, the child needs medicine that you do not have in the medicine kit. Refer a child with 
blood in the stool.  
 Fever for last 7 days or more  
Most fevers go away within a few days. Fever that has lasted for 7 days or more can me an 
that the child has a severe disease. The fever does not have to occur every day, all the time. 
Refer a child who has had fever for the last 7 days or more .  
 Convulsions  
A convulsion during the child’s current illness is a danger sign. A serious infect ion or a high 
fever may be the cause of the convulsion. The health facility can provide the appropriate

diagnosis.diagnosis: Based on the medical guide information provided, the patient's symptoms of diarrhea for 1 day without any mention of blood in the stool could be a case of acute diarrhea. The guide does not provide specific conditions that explain the symptoms beyond the general categorization of diarrhea without blood in the stool as a condition that can often stop on its own in 3 or 4 days. It does not specify causes or likely conditions beyond this description.
diagnosis.treatment:
Immediate Care Steps:
For a patient with Diarrhea for 1 day, the immediate care steps according to the medical guide are as follows:

1. Give ORS (Oral Rehydration Salts) solution. Help the caregiver give the child ORS solution in front of you until the child is no longer thirsty.
2. Give the caregiver 2 ORS packets to take home. Advise the caregiver to give as much as the child wants, but at least ½ cup ORS solution after each loose stool.
3. Give a zinc supplement. The dosage depends on the age of the child:
   - If the child is aged 2 months up to 6 months, give ½ tablet daily for 10 days (total 5 tablets).
   - If the child is aged 6 months up to 5 years, give 1 tablet daily for 10 days (total 10 tablets).
4. Help the caregiver to give the first dose of zinc supplement now.

Home Care:
For a patient with diarrhea for 1 day, the following home care instructions should be provided:

1. Give ORS (Oral Rehydration Solution) to the child. Help the caregiver give the child ORS solution in front of you until the child is no longer thirsty.

2. Give the caregiver 2 ORS packets to take home. Advise the caregiver to give as much ORS solution as the child wants, but at least ½ cup ORS solution after each loose stool.

3. Give zinc supplement. The dosage depends on the age of the child:
   - If the child is aged 2 months up to 6 months, give ½ tablet daily for 10 days (total 5 tablets).
   - If the child is aged 6 months up to 5 years, give 1 tablet daily for 10 days (total 10 tablets).

The medical guide does not provide specific referral criteria for a patient with diarrhea for 1 day.

  Diarrhoea for 14 days or more  
Diarrhoea often stops on its own in 3 or 4 days. Diarrhoea for 14 days or more, however, is a 
danger sign. It may be a sign of a severe disease. The diarrhoea will contribute to malnutrition. 
Diarrhoea also can cause dehydration, when the body loses more f luids than are being 
replaced. If not treated, dehydration results in death. Refer a child with diarrhoea for 14 
days or more.  
 
 Blood in stool  
Diarrhoea with blood in the stool, with or without mucus, is dysentery. If there is blood in the 
stool, the child needs medicine that you do not have in the medicine kit. Refer a child with 
blood in the stool.

Most fevers go away within a few days. Fever that has lasted for 7 days or more can me an 
that the child has a severe disease. The fever does not have to occur every day, all the time. 
Refer a child who has had fever for the last 7 days or more .

fever may be the cause of the convulsion. The health facility can provide the appropriate

4. Check the caregiver’s understanding. For example:  
 Observe to see that she is giving small sips of the ORS 
solution. The child should not choke.  
 Ask her: How often will you give the ORS solution  at home ? 
How much will you give?  
 
5. The child should also  drink the usual fluids that the child drinks, such as breast milk .  
If the child is not exclusively breastfed, the caregiver should offer the child clean water. 
Advise the caregiver not to give very sweet drinks  and juices  to the child with diarrhoea  
who is taking ORS . 
6. How do you know when the child can go home?  
A dehydrated child, who has enough strength to drink, drinks eagerly. If the child 
continues to want to drink the ORS solution, have the mother continue to give the ORS 
solution in front of you . 
If the child becomes more alert and begins to refuse to  drink the ORS, it is likely that the 
child is not dehydrated. If you see that the child is no longer thirsty, then the child is ready 
to go home.  
 
7. Put the extra ORS solution in a container and give it to the caregiver for the trip home (or 
to the health facility, if the child needs to be referred).

solution after each loose stool.  
4. Check the caregiver’s understanding. For example:  
 Observe to see that she is giving small sips of the ORS 
solution. The child should not choke.  
 Ask her: How often will you give the ORS solution  at home ? 
How much will you give?  
 
5. The child should also  drink the usual fluids that the child drinks, such as breast milk .  
If the child is not exclusively breastfed, the caregiver should offer the child clean water. 
Advise the caregiver not to give very sweet drinks  and juices  to the child with diarrhoea  
who is taking ORS . 
6. How do you know when the child can go home?  
A dehydrated child, who has enough strength to drink, drinks eagerly. If the child 
continues to want to drink the ORS solution, have the mother continue to give the ORS 
solution in front of you . 
If the child becomes more alert and begins to refuse to  drink the ORS, it is likely that the 
child is not dehydrated. If you see that the child is no longer thirsty, then the child is ready 
to go home.  
 
7. Put the extra ORS solution in a container and give it to the caregiver for the trip home (or 
to the health facility, if the child needs to be referred).

k child in the community  39 
  Diarrhoea for 14 days or more  
Diarrhoea often stops on its own in 3 or 4 days. Diarrhoea for 14 days or more, however, is a 
danger sign. It may be a sign of a severe disease. The diarrhoea will contribute to malnutrition. 
Diarrhoea also can cause dehydration, when the body loses more f luids than are being 
replaced. If not treated, dehydration results in death. Refer a child with diarrhoea for 14 
days or more.  
 
 Blood in stool  
Diarrhoea with blood in the stool, with or without mucus, is dysentery. If there is blood in the 
stool, the child needs medicine that you do not have in the medicine kit. Refer a child with 
blood in the stool.  
 Fever for last 7 days or more  
Most fevers go away within a few days. Fever that has lasted for 7 days or more can me an 
that the child has a severe disease. The fever does not have to occur every day, all the time. 
Refer a child who has had fever for the last 7 days or more .  
 Convulsions  
A convulsion during the child’s current illness is a danger sign. A serious infect ion or a high 
fever may be the cause of the convulsion. The health facility can provide the appropriate

  Diarrhoea  (3 or more loose stools in 24 hours)  
If the child has diarrhoea, ask: “For how long?”  
Use words the caregiver understands. For example, ask whether the child has had loose or 
watery stools . If yes, then ask how many times a day. It is diarrhoea when there are 3 or more 
loose or watery stools in a 24 -hour day. Frequent passing of  normal, formed stools is not 
diarrhoea.   
 IF DIARRHOEA, Blood in stool  
If the child has diarrhoea, ask: “Is there blood in the stool?”  Check the caregiver’s 
understanding of what blood in stool looks like.  
 Fever  (now or in the last 3 days)  
Identify fever by the  caregiver’s report or by feeling the child. For the caregiver’s report, ask: 
“Does the child have fever now or  did the child have fever  anytime during the last 3 days ?”  
You ask about fever anytime during the last 3 days because fever may not be present

diagnosis.diagnosis: Based on the medical guide information provided, Juanita's symptoms of diarrhea for 2 days without blood in the stool might be a case of acute diarrhea, which is common and often stops on its own in 3 or 4 days. The guide does not provide specific causes for this condition but indicates that diarrhea for less than 14 days and without blood in the stool should be managed with Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS) and zinc supplementation. The symptoms of dehydration, such as dry mouth and decreased urination, are consistent with the effects of diarrhea as described in the guide, which can cause the body to lose more fluids than are being replaced. The guide does not provide information on the specific cause of the cough and difficulty in breathing that Juanita is experiencing.
diagnosis.treatment:
Immediate Care Steps:
For a patient with diarrhea for 2 days, the immediate care steps according to the medical guide are as follows:

1. Give ORS (Oral Rehydration Salts) solution. Help the caregiver give the child ORS solution in front of you until the child is no longer thirsty.
2. Give the caregiver 2 ORS packets to take home. Advise the caregiver to give as much as the child wants, but at least ½ cup ORS solution after each loose stool.
3. Give a zinc supplement. The dosage depends on the age of the child:
   - If the child is aged 2 months up to 6 months, give ½ tablet daily for 10 days (total 5 tablets).
   - If the child is aged 6 months up to 5 years, give 1 tablet daily for 10 days (total 10 tablets).
4. Help the caregiver to give the first dose of the zinc supplement now.

Additionally, if the child

Home Care:
For a patient with diarrhea for 2 days, the following home care instructions should be provided:

1. Give ORS (Oral Rehydration Salts) solution: Help the caregiver give the child ORS solution in front of you until the child is no longer thirsty. Advise the caregiver to give as much as the child wants, but at least ½ cup ORS solution after each loose stool.

2. Provide zinc supplement: Give 1 dose daily for 10 days. The dosage depends on the child's age:
   - Age 2 months up to 6 months: ½ tablet (total 5 tablets)
   - Age 6 months up to 5 years: 1 tablet (total 10 tablets)
   Help the caregiver to give the first dose now.

Referral Guidance:
The medical guide does not provide specific referral criteria for a patient with diarrhea for only 2 days.

  Diarrhoea for 14 days or more  
Diarrhoea often stops on its own in 3 or 4 days. Diarrhoea for 14 days or more, however, is a 
danger sign. It may be a sign of a severe disease. The diarrhoea will contribute to malnutrition. 
Diarrhoea also can cause dehydration, when the body loses more f luids than are being 
replaced. If not treated, dehydration results in death. Refer a child with diarrhoea for 14 
days or more.  
 
 Blood in stool  
Diarrhoea with blood in the stool, with or without mucus, is dysentery. If there is blood in the 
stool, the child needs medicine that you do not have in the medicine kit. Refer a child with 
blood in the stool.  
 Fever for last 7 days or more  
Most fevers go away within a few days. Fever that has lasted for 7 days or more can me an 
that the child has a severe disease. The fever does not have to occur every day, all the time. 
Refer a child who has had fever for the last 7 days or more .  
 Convulsions  
A convulsion during the child’s current illness is a danger sign. A serious infect ion or a high 
fever may be the cause of the convulsion. The health facility can provide the appropriate

  Diarrhoea for 14 days or more  
Diarrhoea often stops on its own in 3 or 4 days. Diarrhoea for 14 days or more, however, is a 
danger sign. It may be a sign of a severe disease. The diarrhoea will contribute to malnutrition. 
Diarrhoea also can cause dehydration, when the body loses more f luids than are being 
replaced. If not treated, dehydration results in death. Refer a child with diarrhoea for 14 
days or more.  
 
 Blood in stool  
Diarrhoea with blood in the stool, with or without mucus, is dysentery. If there is blood in the 
stool, the child needs medicine that you do not have in the medicine kit. Refer a child with 
blood in the stool.  
 Fever for last 7 days or more  
Most fevers go away within a few days. Fever that has lasted for 7 days or more can me an 
that the child has a severe disease. The fever does not have to occur every day, all the time. 
Refer a child who has had fever for the last 7 days or more .  
 Convulsions  
A convulsion during the child’s current illness is a danger sign. A serious infect ion or a high 
fever may be the cause of the convulsion. The health facility can provide the appropriate

5. The child should also  drink the usual fluids that the child drinks, such as breast milk .

6. How do you know when the child can go home?  
A dehydrated child, who has enough strength to drink, drinks eagerly. If the child 
continues to want to drink the ORS solution, have the mother continue to give the ORS 
solution in front of you . 
If the child becomes more alert and begins to refuse to  drink the ORS, it is likely that the 
child is not dehydrated. If you see that the child is no longer thirsty, then the child is ready 
to go home.

diagnosis.diagnosis: The medical guide does not provide specific conditions that directly explain the patient's symptoms of rapid or difficulty breathing. However, it mentions looking for signs of illness such as chest indrawing and fast breathing, which are associated with respiratory distress. These signs require skill and practice to identify and could indicate a serious health issue that needs further assessment. The guide does not specify the conditions these symptoms might indicate, so further information from the guide or additional medical evaluation would be necessary to determine the exact cause of the patient's symptoms.
diagnosis.treatment:
Immediate Care Steps:
The guide doesn't provide specific immediate care steps for a patient case because no patient case has been loaded. Please provide a patient case for tailored advice based on the medical guide information.

Home Care:
Based on the medical guide information provided, for all children treated at home, the following home care advice should be given to the caregiver:

1. Advise the caregiver to give more fluids and continue feeding the child.
2. Advise on when to return to the nearest health facility immediately, or if not possible, return to the Community Health Worker (CHW) if the child:
   - Cannot drink or feed
   - Becomes sicker
   - Has blood in the stool

3. Advise the caregiver on the importance of the child sleeping under a bednet (Insecticide-Treated Net - ITN).
4. Follow up with the child in 3 days (schedule an appointment).

This guidance is essential for ensuring the well-being of the child while being treated at home and provides clear instructions on when further medical help should be sought.

Referral Guidance:
The medical guide does not provide specific referral criteria or conditions for when a patient should be referred to a higher level facility based on the provided excerpts. It mentions the importance of writing a referral note, arranging transportation, and solving other difficulties in referral but does not specify the conditions or warning signs that necessitate such a referral.

 Look for signs of illness (continued)  
 Fast breathing  
Another sign of pneumonia is fast breathing. To look for fast breathing, count the child’s 
breaths for one full minute.  Count the breaths of all children with cough . 
Tell the caregiver you are going to count her child’s breathing. Ask her to keep her child calm. 
If the child is sleeping, do not wake the child.  
The child must be quiet and calm when you count breaths. If the child is frightened, crying, 
angry, or moving  around, you will not be able to do an accurate count.   
 
Choose a place on the child’s chest or stomach where you can easily see the body move as 
the child breathes in. To count the breaths in one minute:   
1. Use a watch with a second hand (or a digital watch , or a timer) . Put the watch in a place 
where you can see the watch and the child’s breathing.  
2. Look for breathing movement anywhere on the child’s chest or stomach.  
Start counting the child’s breaths when the child is calm. Start when the second

diagnosis.diagnosis: Based on the medical guide information provided, if a patient has a cough for 7 days, it does not meet the criteria for the danger sign of a cough lasting 14 days or more. Therefore, without additional symptoms or danger signs listed in the guide (such as diarrhea for 14 days or more, blood in stool, convulsions, fever for the last 7 days or more, not able to drink or feed anything, vomits everything, chest indrawing, unusually sleepy or unconscious, red on MUAC strap, or swelling of both feet), it is not possible to determine specific likely conditions from the information given. The guide does not provide specific diagnoses based on a cough lasting less than 14 days without other symptoms or danger signs.
diagnosis.treatment:
Immediate Care Steps:
The medical guide does not provide specific immediate care steps for a patient with a cough for 7 days. It only specifies that a cough for 14 days or more is a danger sign that requires referral to a health facility for further assessment and treatment. For coughs not reaching the 14-day mark, the guide does not offer direct instructions. Therefore, based on the provided information, no immediate care steps can be recommended for a cough lasting 7 days according to the guide's instructions.

Home Care:
For a patient with a cough for 7 days, the medical guide recommends the following home care instructions:

- Advise the caregiver not to give cough medicine to the child. Cough medicines are expensive and often contain ingredients that are harmful for children.
- Warm water with honey will be comforting and will be all that the child needs.
- Increase the frequency of breastfeeding and breastfeed for longer at each feed to help your baby regain health and weight. If the baby is too weak to suckle, express breast milk to give to the baby. This will also help to keep up your milk supply and prevent breast difficulties.
- For children who are not exclusively breastfed, give clean water and more fluid foods. Soup, rice water, and yoghurt drinks will help to replace the lost fluid during illness.
- The child with diarrhea should also take ORS solution.

Additionally, advise the caregiver to go to the nearest health facility if the child:
- Cannot drink or feed
- Becomes s

Referral Guidance:
Based on the medical guide information provided, a child should be referred to a higher level facility if they have a cough for 14 days or more. Since the patient in question has a cough for 7 days, according to the guide's specific referral criteria, they would not be referred based on the cough duration alone. The guide does not provide specific referral criteria for a cough of 7 days.

When you are sick, you can continue to breastfeed your baby. You may need extra fluids and

A child with cough may also have a sore throat. A sore throat is uncomfortable and can 
prevent the child from drinki ng and feeding well. Advise the caregiver not to give cough 
medicine to a child. Cough medicines are expensive. And they often contain ingredients that 
are harmful for children. Warm water with honey will be comforting. It will be all that the child 
needs.   
  Advise on when to return  
Advise the caregiver to go to the nearest health facility if the child becomes sicker. This 
means that the medicine is not working or the child has another problem.  
Emphasize that it is urgent to seek care immediately if the child:  
 Cannot drink or feed  
 Becomes sicker  
 Has blood in the stool  
Usually a caregiver will know when a child is improving or becoming sicker. Ask the caregiver 
what she will look for. A child may become weaker and very sleepy. A child with a cough may 
have  difficulty  breathing.  Make sure that the caregiver recognizes when the child is not

baby regain health and weight.

breast when offered.

When you are sick, you can continue to breastfeed your baby. You may need extra fluids and

A child with cough may also have a sore throat. A sore throat is uncomfortable and can 
prevent the child from drinki ng and feeding well. Advise the caregiver not to give cough 
medicine to a child. Cough medicines are expensive. And they often contain ingredients that 
are harmful for children. Warm water with honey will be comforting. It will be all that the child 
needs.

means that the medicine is not working or the child has another problem.  
Emphasize that it is urgent to seek care immediately if the child:

 Becomes sicker  
 Has blood in the stool

If the baby is too weak to suckle, express breast milk to give the baby. This will also help you 
to keep  up your milk supply and prevent breast difficulties.  
Increase the frequency of breastfeeding and breastfeed for longer at each feed , to help your 
baby regain health and weight.  
Do not give any throat or cough remedy. A child, even with a sore throat, will usually take the 
breast when offered.  
When you are sick, you can continue to breastfeed your baby. You may need extra fluids and  
support during this time yourself.  
For children who are not exclusively breastfed, give clean water and more fluid foods. Soup, 
rice water, and yoghurt drinks will help to replace the lost fluid during illness. The child with 
diarrhoea should also take ORS solution.
